title: Are secret marriages (nikah) permissible in Islam?
tags: is nikah valid without telling anyone
does nikah have to be announced
stanceSummary: A secret marriage — where only the witnesses and the couple know, with others actively told to keep it secret — is **technically valid** if the minimum conditions are met (wali for the previously unmarried woman, two witnesses, mahr). However, it is **strongly discouraged (makruh) and goes against the spirit of the Sharia**. No marriage will flourish in such conditions. It is especially harmful to the woman. Yasir Qadhi says plainly: 'Be a man and get married properly.' The bare technical minimum is not the Islamic ideal — it is the lowest permissible bar.
alternateQuestions: Is a secret nikah valid in Islam?
Can a Muslim get married without telling their family?
Does a nikah have to be announced or made public?
Is it halal to do a nikah with only two witnesses and tell no one?
What is the ruling on secret marriages in Islam?
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2025 Q&A session video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OwE4Qd3I2vs) (starting at [1:15:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OwE4Qd3I2vs&t=4500s)), Yasir Qadhi addresses the growing trend of secret marriages in Muslim communities.

- A secret nikah is **technically valid** if the minimum conditions are fulfilled: a wali (for a previously unmarried woman), two witnesses, and mahr
- However, it is **strongly discouraged (makruh)** and goes against the entire spirit of the Islamic marriage contract
- Telling witnesses to keep the marriage secret directly contradicts the purpose of the nikah, which is a **public communal event**, not a private transaction
- Such marriages invariably cause harm — especially to the woman — and will not produce the lasting happiness that nikah is meant to bring
- Yasir Qadhi's direct message to men: **"Be a man and get married properly"** — you don't need a 5,000-person wedding, but you should not be keeping your marriage hidden
- Technical halal is the **lowest bar** — a marriage cannot flourish at the bare minimum level on any dimension

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### [1:15:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OwE4Qd3I2vs&t=4500s)

A question was raised: secret marriages are becoming more and more common in our community — where only the witnesses and the couple know, and the witnesses are asked not to tell anyone. What is the Islamic ruling on this?

Yasir Qadhi responds directly: forget the technicalities for a moment. **No marriage will flourish if it is done in secret.** This is not ethical. It is not noble. It will not lead to long-lasting happiness.

If you wish to get married, **be a man and get married properly.** Go to the wali of the woman and do not have any secrets. You do not need a massive wedding of thousands of people — but you cannot, and should not, tell the witnesses: "Hey, keep this secret." That contradicts the entire spirit of the nikah.

### The Technical Question — [1:16:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OwE4Qd3I2vs&t=4560s)

The problem, Yasir Qadhi acknowledges, is that people ask: **is this technically halal?** And here is the answer: yes, if you meet the bare minimum conditions — a wali (for a previously unmarried woman), two witnesses, and a mahr — the contract is technically valid and there may be no sin in the eyes of Allah.

But there is no dignity in such a marriage. There is no nobility. And it will cause harm to the individuals involved, especially the woman.

### The Principle — [1:17:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OwE4Qd3I2vs&t=4620s)

Here is the key lesson: **technical halal is the lowest level of the Sharia, not the goal.** If a husband gave his wife the bare minimum of attention, time, love, and fulfillment of her rights — and she gave him the bare minimum of her marital duties — that marriage would completely fall apart. The Sharia sets a minimum; it does not describe the ideal.

The same applies here. A secret marriage may avoid the category of *haram*, but it is **strongly discouraged (makruh)** at the very least. It should not become the norm. It goes against the spirit of a nikah, which is a communal, family-based, publicly known commitment.

For the women especially: the wali exists for your protection. When the wali is brought into the picture, the dynamic of the relationship changes completely. A man who is serious and respectful will have no problem involving the wali. Secrecy is often a sign that something is wrong with the proposal. Be cautious.

And Allah knows best.
